result: I keep thinking about the way light pools on my kitchen floor at 5:17 p.m., a spilled gold that disappears if I blink too long. It’s the same light that once slid across my grandmother’s linoleum in 1993, coaxing me into believing time is a circle we walk through once, then again, barefoot and forgetful. I am thirty-eight and still tasting the ghost of her blackberry jam, still hearing the pressure-cooker hiss like a locomotive impatient for the future. Yesterday I caught myself whispering the recipe out loud—four cups of crushed fruit, the lemon-juice splash that smells like bottled summer—while I stared at a screen full of deadlines. The juxtaposition felt obscene, like holding a hymn in one hand and a grenade in the other. Yet both fit the palm the same way. I wonder if the data I compress and upload each evening will ever ferment into something sweet, something that can be spread across toast twenty years from now when my own nephew stands in a stranger’s kitchen chasing light with his tongue. Perhaps memory is just another preservation method: sugar, acid, airtight seal. Perhaps we are all mason jars lined on a dusty shelf, waiting for someone hungry enough to pry us open, to taste the bright dark inside us and say, yes, this is what the year felt like when the world slowed and the light arrived right on time, every day, without postage or apology, simply because the planet tilts and the sun insists and we, miraculously, are still here to notice.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
